content.problemStatement:
Concatenation of two integers is obtained as follows: First, convert both integers to strings. Then concatenate both strings into one and convert this concatenated string back to integer.

For example, concatenation of 666 and 777 is CONC(6,7)=67CONC(6, 7) = 67CONC(6,7)=67, concatenation of 123123123 and 454545 is CONC(123,45)=12345CONC(123, 45) = 12345CONC(123,45)=12345.

You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN integers. You are also given two integers LLL and RRR. Find the number of pairs (iii, jjj) such that  1≤i,j≤N1 \leq i, j \leq N1≤i,j≤N and   L≤CONC(Ai,Aj)≤RL \leq CONC(A_i, A_j) ≤ RL≤CONC(Ai​,Aj​)≤R

**Note:** Since the size of the input and output is large, please use fast input-output methods.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then TTT test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains three integers N,L,RN, L, RN,L,R.
The second line of each test case line contains NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2,\dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line the number of suitable pairs.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5 per test case, sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow.
For each test case:
- The first line contains three integers N, L and R (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5, 1 ≤ L ≤ R ≤ 10^15).
- The second line contains N integers A1, A2, …, AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^7).
All numbers are separated by single spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of ordered pairs (i, j) (1 ≤ i, j ≤ N) such that L ≤ CONCAT(Ai, Aj) ≤ R, where CONCAT(x, y) is the integer obtained by writing the decimal representation of x followed by that of y.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1:


(i=1,j=1)(i = 1, j = 1)(i=1,j=1): CONC(A1,A1)=22CONC(A_1, A_1) = 22CONC(A1​,A1​)=22 and 10≤22≤5210 \leq 22 \leq 5210≤22≤52.


(i=1,j=2)(i = 1, j = 2)(i=1,j=2): CONC(A1,A2)=25CONC(A_1, A_2) = 25CONC(A1​,A2​)=25 and 10≤25≤5210 \leq 25 \leq 5210≤25≤52.


(i=2,j=1)(i = 2, j = 1)(i=2,j=1): CONC(A2,A1)=52CONC(A_2, A_1) = 52CONC(A2​,A1​)=52 and 10≤52≤5210 \leq 52 \leq 5210≤52≤52.


(i=2,j=2)(i = 2, j = 2)(i=2,j=2): CONC(A2,A2)=55CONC(A_2, A_2) = 55CONC(A2​,A2​)=55 and 10≤5510 \leq 5510≤55 but 55≰52 55 \nleq 5255≰52.


So there are three suitable pairs.
Test case 2: There is no suitable pair.
Test case 3: The suitable pairs are (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1) and (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2).

solution.approach:
For a number x and a length d, CONCAT(x, y) = x·10^d + y where d = number of decimal digits of y. The length of any Ai is at most 8 because Ai ≤ 10^7.  

Pre‑process the array by grouping numbers according to their digit length. For each length d (1 … 8) store all Ai having exactly d digits in a separate vector and sort it.  

For each element Ai (treated as the left part of the concatenation) we iterate over all possible lengths d of the right part. Let pow = 10^d. The concatenated value lies in [L, R] iff
    L ≤ Ai·pow + Aj ≤ R  ⇔  L - Ai·pow ≤ Aj ≤ R - Ai·pow.
Thus for the current d we need to count how many numbers in the sorted vector of length d fall into the interval [low, high] where low = L - Ai·pow and high = R - Ai·pow. This can be done with two binary searches (lower_bound and upper_bound). The count is added to the answer.  
The procedure is repeated for every Ai and every possible d, giving O(N·D·log N) time where D ≤ 8, which easily fits the limits (≈ 8·10^6·log10^5 operations). Memory consumption is O(N) for the stored vectors.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N · D · log N) per test case, where D ≤ 8 (the maximum number of digit lengths).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the numbers split by digit length.
